title: Speech Quirks & Verbal Tics
description: A distinctive pattern in how this NPC speaks — pace, vocabulary, habit, or structure.

entries.result: They answer questions with a question, not evasively but reflexively — as if the question needs to be examined before the answer can be found.
They begin sentences with 'The thing is —' and then say the thing, which is usually the actual point and always takes a moment to arrive.
They use very old-fashioned or formal vocabulary in casual conversation, seemingly without noticing the register mismatch.
They speak in complete, well-formed sentences at all times — no fragments, no interruptions of themselves, no trailing off.
They trail off at the end of sentences, letting the last few words go quiet, as if the conclusion is available if you care to find it.
They punctuate statements with 'you understand' or 'you follow' — not as condescension, but as a genuine check that the communication is landing.
They narrate their own thinking aloud — 'So that means...', 'Which would suggest...' — working through things verbally and in public.
They use the names of people they're talking about far more often than is natural — full names, consistently, even in the same sentence.
They pause for an uncomfortable length of time before speaking. Not hesitation — deliberation. The answer will come, and it will be considered.
They speak very quickly when interested and very slowly when skeptical, the pace a reliable barometer.
They disagree by first restating the other person's position more clearly than the person stated it, then dismantling it carefully.
They use profanity sparingly and with surgical precision — one word, the right word, in the right moment, which makes it land considerably harder.
They never use profanity under any circumstances, which becomes its own kind of emphasis in moments of stress.
They repeat the last word someone said before responding — very quietly, almost to themselves — as if confirming receipt.
They speak with excessive precision about quantities and times — not 'a few days ago' but 'eleven days ago', not 'some' but 'roughly forty percent'.
They hedge every claim with qualifications — 'it seems to me', 'as far as I can tell', 'I may be wrong but' — regardless of how certain they actually are.
They make no qualifications whatsoever. Every statement is delivered with identical flat certainty, which makes uncertainty invisible.
They give compliments that are technically accurate and somehow slightly off — the right observation, applied at slightly the wrong angle.
They deliver criticism as questions — 'Did you consider that the approach might be... inefficient?' — in a way that is more cutting than a direct statement.
They laugh at their own jokes slightly before finishing them, which is either endearing or irritating depending on whether the jokes are good.
They don't laugh at other people's jokes — a polite smile, if that — and then move on at exactly the right pace.
They use a single recurring metaphor from their past work or life for almost everything — ships for plans, weather for moods, construction for relationships.
They avoid metaphors entirely, finding them imprecise, and will occasionally say so.
They tell stories to make points, always, even when a direct statement would be faster — the story is the point, not just an illustration.
They make their point once, clearly, and do not repeat it — if it wasn't understood, the problem is the listener's.
They repeat themselves, not from forgetfulness but from a need to be sure the thing was heard — the same statement, different words, until the other person confirms.
They use 'we' when they mean 'I', distributing responsibility or credit into a collective that may or may not exist.
They refer to themselves in the third person occasionally, particularly when talking about their own past decisions.
They begin most substantive statements with a small sound — a breath, a click, a very quiet hm — that serves as a verbal on-ramp.
They end statements with a rising inflection that sounds like a question but isn't one — it's an invitation to respond, not a request for confirmation.
They speak in very short sentences when they want something and very long ones when they're explaining why they want it.
They have a specific filler word — 'essentially', 'fundamentally', 'naturally', 'obviously' — that appears between thoughts with no apparent purpose.
They use 'of course' to introduce information the other person clearly didn't know, which functions as a very subtle form of condescension.
They say 'fair enough' to close topics they disagree with, conveying acceptance without agreement in three syllables.
They over-explain context — providing more background than any particular conversation requires, as if each exchange is the first one.
They skip context entirely, assuming shared knowledge that may not exist, then seem mildly surprised when clarification is needed.
They whisper for emphasis, not volume — the quieter they get, the more important the thing being said.
They get louder for emphasis in a way that is not quite raising their voice but is noticably more present.
They use silence as punctuation — a full beat after a key point, before continuing, that forces the listener to sit with the statement.
They fill silence immediately, reflexively, the way some people fill space with furniture — the quiet is uncomfortable and must be addressed.
They address the most difficult part of a topic first and work outward from it — the opposite of most conversational instincts.
They address the least difficult part of a topic first and work slowly toward the center, which they may or may not actually reach.
They use people's names at the start of sentences when they want attention and at the end when they want agreement.
They never use names at all, which in a long conversation becomes oddly intimate.
They speak about past events as if they happened to someone else — third person, slightly distanced, clinically.
They speak about past events with immediate present-tense vividness — 'and then I turn around and there he is' — pulling you into the scene.
They qualify praise excessively — 'quite good', 'not bad', 'better than I expected' — so that unqualified approval, when it comes, is remarkable.
They give unqualified approval generously and frequently, which either means they mean it or it means very little.
They ask for clarification even when clarification isn't needed, as if buying time to formulate a response.
They never ask for clarification, preferring to proceed on their interpretation and correct course later if necessary.
They have a phrase they use when they want a subject dropped — 'there it is', 'so it goes', 'that's that' — and they use it with some finality.
They transition between topics with no verbal bridge, the conversation moving in jumps that require you to keep up.
They transition between topics with elaborate verbal bridges — 'which brings me to', 'on a related note', 'speaking of which' — as if narrating their own conversation.
They speak about absent people with the same directness they use for people in the room, no lowering of voice, no change of register.
They lower their voice when speaking about absent people, regardless of who else is present, as a matter of private principle.
They credit sources — 'I heard this from someone who would know', 'someone told me once' — even when the information is clearly their own experience.
They never credit sources, presenting all information as if arrived at independently.
They say 'I'm going to be honest with you' before certain statements, which retroactively makes everything else sound slightly dishonest.
They say 'I won't lie' as a sentence opener, which functions identically.
They begin disagreements with 'Help me understand' and mean it — they genuinely want the explanation before the argument.
They don't signal disagreement verbally at all — they simply ask a series of increasingly specific questions until the flaw surfaces on its own.
They use humor to deflect uncomfortable topics, reliably, with some skill — the joke lands and the topic is gone.
They become extremely literal when uncomfortable, parsing words precisely when the emotional content is what's actually being discussed.
They go quiet when they're angry — not silence out of control, but the deliberate kind, where the speaking will resume and it will be measured.
They become unusually voluble when anxious — more words, faster pace, more detail than the situation requires.
They swear a great deal in casual conversation and not at all when they're serious, which makes the absence of profanity its own signal.
They have a habit of summarizing what the other person just said before responding — a genuine compression, not a paraphrase to argue against.
They interrupt only to agree, never to contradict — the interruption is always additive.
They interrupt rarely, but when they do it is to correct a factual error, and they do it without apology.
They do not interrupt at all, under any circumstances, which sometimes means the conversation has moved past the point by the time they respond.
They have a specific rhetorical structure for bad news — context, then event, then implication — and they deploy it consistently.
They deliver bad news the same way they deliver any other news, with no preparation or softening, which is either refreshing or brutal depending on the news.
They use 'apparently' for things they know to be true, which gives their conversation a permanent air of mild skepticism about their own information.
They describe things they're certain about with the language of uncertainty and things they're uncertain about with the language of fact.
They say 'interesting' when they mean 'wrong', 'concerning', 'alarming', or 'I disagree strongly but need a moment'.
They have a verbal catch they use when they've made a decision: 'Right then', 'There we are', 'Done' — a closing phrase that is also a signal.
They check in constantly during explanation — 'with me so far?', 'does that make sense?' — with genuine concern that the explanation is working.
They never check in during explanation, delivering the whole thing and then waiting for questions.
They use diminutives — calling things 'a bit', 'a touch', 'a shade' — that systematically understate the scale of whatever they're describing.
They use amplifiers — 'significantly', 'considerably', 'substantially' — that consistently overstate, giving their speech a permanent sense of drama.
They apologize before saying difficult things and not at all afterward.
They don't apologize before difficult things but offer a kind of practical acknowledgment afterward — 'that was a lot' — which functions similarly.
They describe emotions through physical symptoms rather than naming them — 'there was this pressure' rather than 'I was afraid'.
They name emotions directly and clinically — 'I was afraid', 'I felt guilty', 'I was angry' — with no metaphor and no softening.
They avoid the word 'I' when discussing things they've done wrong, which produces a lot of passive constructions and impersonal framings.
They use 'one' instead of 'you' or 'I' in moments of self-disclosure — 'one tends to find', 'one notices' — a distancing habit.
They speak about the future in the conditional — 'if things go as expected', 'assuming nothing changes' — a constitutionally cautious relationship with prediction.
They speak about the future in the present tense — 'next week I arrive', 'tomorrow we have this conversation' — as if it is already scheduled.
They retell stories slightly differently each time, not dishonestly but organically — the version fitting the audience, the details shifting.
They tell stories identically every time, same words, same emphasis, same pauses — the version has been fixed and will not change.
They ask 'Does that make sense?' but it is not a question — it's a conversational full stop, and they are already moving on.
They never ask if they've been understood. If you didn't follow, that will emerge in time.
They have a very particular word they use to mean 'that's wrong and we should stop' — a private flag that people who know them recognize immediately.
They use technical vocabulary from their profession or background in all conversations regardless of whether the other person shares it.
They deliberately avoid technical vocabulary even when it would be more precise, preferring accessible language even if it's less exact.
They frame requests as observations — 'I notice the door is open', 'it seems like the report isn't finished' — and wait to see if the observation is acted upon.
They make requests very directly, which is efficient and occasionally startles people who are used to indirection.
They never ask for things they want, preferring to create conditions in which the thing will be offered.
They have a habit of the long preamble — context, background, qualification — before arriving at a short and simple point.
They make the point first and provide context only when asked, which can feel abrupt but is extremely efficient.
They say 'to be fair' to introduce points that favor people they disagree with, which is both generous and slightly exhausting.
They speak differently to different people — not dishonestly, but adaptively — vocabulary, pace, and register shifting to meet whoever they're with.
They speak exactly the same way to everyone, regardless of context or audience, which is either admirable consistency or a failure of social calibration.
They give affirmations while listening — 'mm', 'right', 'yes' — at a rate and pace that actually confirms they're present.
They listen completely silently, no affirmations, no nods, which can feel like absence but isn't.
They use stories from history — distant, often apocryphal — as illustrations, the moral applied loosely enough to fit most situations.
They use examples from their own experience exclusively, which is either refreshing or exhausting depending on how interesting their life has been.
They speak about themselves in the past tense even for recent events, as if their own biography is already closed.
They rarely refer to themselves at all — the conversation is about the subject, not about their relationship to the subject.
They have a phrase for when they've changed their mind — 'having thought about it', 'on reflection' — that they deploy without embarrassment.
They never explicitly change their mind in conversation, the update arriving as a slightly different emphasis next time.
They say 'that's a good question' only when it actually is one, which means the phrase carries real weight.
They say 'that's a good question' for every question, which means the phrase carries no weight at all.
They answer the question they wish had been asked when the actual question isn't quite right.
They answer exactly and only the question asked, nothing more, which can feel cooperative or evasive in identical measure.
They have a physical gesture that accompanies a specific word or phrase — a flat hand for 'on the other hand', a raised finger for 'specifically'.
They talk to themselves quietly when working or thinking alone, and don't quite stop when others enter the room.
They go completely silent when thinking, and the silence is productive and not uncomfortable to be around.
They use 'obviously' for things that are not obvious, having long since stopped noticing that their baseline is not everyone's baseline.
They use 'perhaps' when they mean 'definitely' and 'certainly' when they mean 'I hope so', a consistent inversion of their actual certainty.
They ask personal questions without preamble — 'are you all right?', 'what happened with that?' — direct, without framing, genuinely interested.
They never ask personal questions unless invited, which means they often know less than they could and carry the gap quietly.
They give warnings as observations — 'people who do that tend to find...' — rather than advice, leaving the application to the listener.
They give advice as direct instruction — 'don't do that', 'here's what you should do' — without framing it as suggestion.
They never give unsolicited advice, which can read as indifference but is actually discipline.
They speak at a pace that is just slightly faster than comfortable, which creates a faint ambient pressure in conversation.
They speak at a pace that is just slightly slower than expected, which creates a faint ambient calm.
They have a verbal habit of completing other people's sentences — not rudely, often correctly — that is difficult to object to but subtly takes over the conversation.
They use 'I think' to introduce their firmest convictions and speak more flatly when they're uncertain — the reverse of the usual signal.
They speak more warmly about people they dislike than about people they like, which makes their genuine enthusiasm, when it appears, very easy to miss.
They close topics physically — a small nod, a shift in posture — as well as verbally, so the end of a subject is visible as well as stated.
They speak entirely in the active voice, which gives their conversation a quality of personal accountability even in neutral description.
They drift into the passive voice for events they were involved in, a grammatical distancing from their own actions.
They use weather metaphors exclusively — 'cold', 'a storm coming', 'the air has cleared' — for emotional and relational states.
They use economic metaphors exclusively — 'cost', 'invest', 'return', 'loss' — for emotional and relational states.
They narrate other people's likely inner states aloud — 'she's probably thinking...', 'he'll want to...' — with a confidence that is sometimes correct.
They never speculate about other people's inner states — only observable behavior, only what was said or done.
They use 'we' when discussing plans they have already made alone — a retroactive invitation into something that is not actually collaborative.
They speak very differently in their first language than in the one they're using — not just vocabulary, but rhythm, directness, and warmth.
They have a word or phrase from another language they use in conversation, untranslated, for concepts they consider untranslatable.
They quote people — accurately, attributed — mid-conversation with a frequency that suggests they experience the world partly as a collection of things said.
They never quote anyone, presenting all ideas as arrived at independently.
They speak about their failures as if they are data — informative, unremarkable, valuable in the way that failed experiments are valuable.
They speak about their failures not at all, which means you know them only from their successes.
They have a habit of recapping — at the end of a conversation, a meeting, an explanation — what was decided or understood, which is useful and slightly clinical.
They end conversations abruptly, with purpose — the business is done, the words are said, the meeting is over.
They end conversations slowly, with several false endings — 'anyway', 'so', 'right' — before the actual goodbye.
They speak about future problems in the past tense — 'when this has gone wrong, what we'll have needed to do is...' — which is a strange but effective planning mode.
They say 'I need you to understand something' before anything genuinely important, which works because they use it so rarely.
They have a tell when they're uncertain: a very specific phrase — 'it's possible that', 'one scenario is' — that maps onto genuine doubt rather than performed qualification.
They use humor only self-deprecatingly, never at anyone else's expense, which is both generous and slightly limiting.
They use humor only at their own expense when they want something — the self-deprecation is a tool.
They respond to compliments with deflection, not false modesty — genuine uncertainty about whether the praise is warranted, visible on their face.
They accept compliments directly and briefly — 'thank you', nothing more — which is either confident or slightly unnerving.
They speak very differently when tired — shorter sentences, more direct, less filtered — and probably don't know this.
They have a habit of stating the obvious before the non-obvious, as if making sure the ground is solid before adding weight to it.
They use 'frankly' before statements that are no more or less frank than their other statements.
They have a voice that changes register based on who has the most power in the room, unconsciously tracking status through tone.
They speak to everyone in exactly the same register regardless of status, which reads as either democratic or oblivious depending on the situation.
They use 'we might consider' to mean 'we should do this', and most people who know them have learned to translate it.
They avoid the word 'no' directly, preferring 'that's difficult', 'I'm not sure that works', 'let me think about it' — all of which mean no.
They say no directly and immediately, which is clear and occasionally startling.
They have a physical tell for when they're lying — not obvious, but there — that anyone who spends time with them will eventually notice.
They have no detectable tell for untruth, which is either because they're very controlled or because they've said difficult things so often the body no longer distinguishes.
They preface disagreements with agreement — 'you're right that..., and also...' — a conversational move that is both generous and tactically effective.
They state their conclusion first and their reasoning second, which is efficient and can feel like a demand for agreement rather than an argument.
They state their reasoning first and their conclusion last, which builds to a point in a way that rewards patience.
They have a phrase they use when they're worried about someone — 'how are you, actually?' or 'are you sleeping?' — that is distinct from their ordinary greeting.
They give instructions by describing the desired outcome rather than the method — 'I need it to be clean', not 'here's how to clean it'.
They give instructions by describing the method very precisely, every step, leaving the outcome to follow correctly from the process.
They use 'to be clear' before statements that need no clarification, and omit it before statements that do.
They have a verbal tic that surfaces when they're bored — a small sound, a specific word, a rhythm — that they are probably unaware of.
They repeat themselves for emphasis — the same phrase twice in quick succession — when they mean it most.
They speak very quietly in moments of genuine urgency, the voice dropping rather than rising, which requires people to lean in.
They introduce themselves with more information than asked for — name, then something else, then something else — as if getting the full picture out quickly.
They introduce themselves with only a name and nothing more, waiting to see what questions follow.
They use 'just' excessively — 'just wondering', 'just thought', 'just checking' — a minimizing habit that undersells what they actually want.
They use 'actually' frequently, in a way that implies mild surprise that the thing is true.
They speak about their past selves in the third person — 'the person I was then', 'that version of me' — a clean division they've drawn and maintained.
They speak about their past selves in the first person with present-tense ownership — 'I did that', 'I made that choice' — no distance, no revision.
They have a habit of speaking in parallel structure — listing things in threes, balancing clauses — that gives their speech a slightly rhetorical quality.
They speak in a way that sounds improvised even when it isn't — no polish, no prepared phrasing, the words arriving as they're needed.
They speak in a way that sounds prepared even when it isn't — clean sentences, structured arguments, no searching for words.
They use 'at the end of the day' to introduce the thing they actually believe, having cleared away all the qualifications.
They speak very precisely about time — durations, sequences, intervals — in a way that suggests time is a resource they track carefully.
They speak very loosely about time — 'a while ago', 'eventually', 'at some point' — as if the sequence is less important than the fact.
They close difficult conversations with a practical next step — not to avoid the emotion, but to give it somewhere to go.
They have a phrase they return to when they're overwhelmed — a sentence, always the same one, that seems to function as a reset.
They speak with a rhythm that makes it easy to listen to them for a long time, something in the pacing and variation that is almost musical.
They speak with a rhythm that requires attention to follow — dense, asymmetrical, the stress landing in unexpected places.
They narrate their own decision-making in real time — 'the question is whether to...', 'on balance I think...' — thinking out loud as a form of transparency.
They make decisions silently and announce them — no visible process, just the outcome — which can feel either decisive or opaque.
They use understatement so consistently that their most alarming statements sound mild and require a beat to land.
They use overstatement so consistently that when they're actually alarmed, it's indistinguishable from their normal register.
They say 'I could be wrong' at the end of their firmest convictions and nowhere else.
They speak more freely to strangers than to people they know well, something in the anonymity loosening them.
They speak less freely to strangers than to anyone they trust, the warmth emerging only as familiarity accumulates.
They have a phrase they use to end conversations that is always the same — not a standard goodbye, something specific — that is either a habit or a small ritual.
They speak about things they care about with visible precision — more words, more specific, more exact — and about things they don't with comfortable vagueness.
They say exactly what they mean and mean exactly what they say, which in most company is remarkable enough to notice.
